inlineContent.text: A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen

Borrowed cursors cannot escape their closure — that is
what makes them allocation-free. When you need to store a
node reference, send it across actors, or iterate in an
order the cursor API does not directly support, promote
ARC retain on the underlying tree storage. Allocate them
for end-of-pipeline storage; do not allocate them per-node

structure is not addressable through it.
When should you reach for an owned
Tree size is not the deciding factor. Borrowed
cursors stay efficient regardless of node count.
Handles cost an ARC retain and an
allocation per call to the owned-traversal
helpers; the trade is worth it once the reference
needs to outlive the borrow scope.
When you need to store the reference, send it across
actors, or pass it to APIs that take owned handles
Borrowed cursors do not stack-allocate per node;
making handles for every token would just churn
For every leaf token, to keep the cursor stack short.

abstract.text: Cambium’s CST stores everything as homogeneous nodes tagged
with a kind. That uniformity is what makes editing and reuse
cheap, but it leaves consumers to write
everywhere they want kind-specific behavior.
The witnesses you built in Tutorial 7 (replacement) and
Tutorial 8 (reparse) exist to make analysis caches
. Without translation every keystroke would invalidate
every memoized value — defeating the whole point of an
incremental tool.
is
. Cambium’s red
layer realizes lazily under lock-free atomic slots, so two
tasks descending into different siblings of the same parent
never observe inconsistent state. The typed AST overlays are
also
, so passing a sub-expression to a
child task is just an argument pass.
Cambium’s CST stores everything as homogeneous nodes tagged
with a kind. That uniformity is what makes editing and reuse
cheap, but it leaves consumers to write
everywhere they want kind-specific behavior.
Cambium is a Swift-native concrete syntax tree library. This
tutorial series walks you through building a small but
production-shaped calculator language end-to-end: a recursive-
descent parser that produces a lossless tree, a typed AST overlay,
an evaluator with memoized analysis, persistent edits with
identity-preserving witnesses, an incremental reparse loop, fork-
join evaluation across structured-concurrent tasks, parallel
parsing with externalized interning, and on-disk serialization.
is

tasks descending into different siblings of the same parent
never observe inconsistent state. The typed AST overlays are
also
, so passing a sub-expression to a
child task is just an argument pass.
Cambium trees are persistent: there is no in-place mutation.

content: // Lifted from CalculatorParser.swift into its own file. We grow it
// here with query helpers built on the borrowed-cursor APIs.
/// What does the parser think about a single byte position?
/// - `.single(token)`: the offset is strictly inside a token.
/// - `.between(left, right)`: the offset is at a token boundary.
/// The closure-per-case shape forces the caller to address every
/// case. The cursors are borrowed; copy out only what you need
/// What is the smallest node or token wholly covering `range`?
/// `withCoveringElement` walks down the tree and stops at the
/// element whose own range contains `range` but none of whose

correctly the parser needs operator precedence; to

abstract.text: Parallel parsing splits work across worker tasks: each
worker parses an independent subtree concurrently, then a
master builder splices the worker results into one logical
tree. The correctness-critical question is how the workers
share token-text vocabulary and the green-node namespace.

/// Whether the source bytes contain `needle` as a UTF-8 substring.
/// `needle.utf8` is materialized as an `Array`, the document
/// Per `SyntaxText`'s contract an empty needle returns `true`.
/// First byte-range matching `needle` in source order.
/// `firstRange(of:)` runs a KMP-style scan over the chunk-by-chunk
/// view that `SyntaxText` exposes — no per-chunk allocation.
/// Bytes covered by `range`, materialized as a `String`. Only the
/// FNV-1a hash of the source bytes, computed by streaming chunks
/// through a custom ``CambiumCore/UTF8Sink``. The sink sees the
/// document one buffer at a time without ever holding it as a
/// `String` — the right shape for hashing, checksums, or any
/// Two parses of identical source produce identical hashes; any
// FNV1aHasher.write does not throw; this branch is
// unreachable. Crash loudly if the contract changes.
/// Every expression-shape node in the tree, in depth-first
/// — copyable references that can be iterated, stored in
/// dictionaries, or sent across actors after the borrowed cursor
/// ``CambiumOwnedTraversal``; it allocates an array. For hot-path
/// scans, prefer borrowed traversal — handles cost ARC traffic
/// Every token in the tree, optionally filtered to a byte range.
/// `tokenHandles(in:)` is the analogous owned-handle helper for
/// A reference `UTF8Sink` conformance: an FNV-1a 64-bit hash that
/// consumes the document's UTF-8 chunks without buffering. The same
/// hash family Cambium uses internally for its green-node identity.
mutating func write(_ bytes: UnsafeBufferPointer<UInt8>) throws {

abstract.text: With the typed AST in place from Tutorial 5, an evaluator
becomes a recursive function that pattern-matches on
. Each kind has a closed structure — its operands
and operator are addressable through the typed accessors —
so the evaluator stays small and total.
Parallel parsing splits work across worker tasks: each
worker parses an independent subtree concurrently, then a
master builder splices the worker results into one logical
tree. The correctness-critical question is how the workers
share token-text vocabulary and the green-node namespace.
Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.

Every Cambium grammar starts with a kind enumeration: one case per
token and one per node the parser can produce. The kind enum
becomes the
associated type of a
conformance, and Cambium specializes its generic types around that
language to make tree storage compact and traversal type-safe.
